title: Constraint conservation task-specific hybrid follow-up

queries: task selective transfer negative transfer structured representation alignment hybrid ablation
per task transfer gating avoid negative transfer multi-domain graph representation
task specific conservative transfer control structured prompt alignment

evidence.why_now: EXP201 and EXP204 together narrowed the Part 3 causal picture into a task-specific split: user-churn regressed badly when Amazon transfer was disabled entirely, while user-ltv improved the most when Amazon transfer was pushed furthest down, and item-incoterms kept the same strong salt-side continuation signal in both bundles. The next justified continuation is therefore a hybrid screen that keeps the salt-side path fixed while assigning each Amazon representative task the narrower setting that best matched its own screening evidence.

hypothesis: If the surviving Part 3 signal is genuinely task-specific across the representative tasks, then keeping user-churn near the EXP201 Amazon gate-only setting, keeping user-ltv near the EXP204 Amazon-off setting, and leaving the stronger salt-side path unchanged should preserve the user-ltv continuation signal without reintroducing the severe user-churn regression seen when Amazon transfer was disabled uniformly.
notes: This is a screening-only hybrid follow-up after EXP201 and EXP204 together suggested a task-specific split across the two Amazon representative tasks.

The intended comparison is narrow and evidence-driven rather than a broad manual hyperparameter scan: user-churn keeps the EXP201 gate-only Amazon setting, user-ltv keeps the EXP204 Amazon-off setting, and the stronger salt-side Part 3 path stays unchanged.
Do not run Optuna or final full test from this candidate directly.
The comparison remains fair because the validated Phase 2 per-task best hyperparameters stay fixed and only the task-specific Part 3 transfer control differs.

Scientific interpretation still follows the Stage 3 task-aware rule set: user-ltv subset/full-test scale mismatch still applies, and item-incoterms should still be judged scientifically against the stored full-test reference band rather than only the optimistic subset baseline.
